channel.title: JLT Daily Dispatch — LinkedIn
channel.description: The JLT Daily Dispatch — LinkedIn Business edition

item.title: Three things to know on the EU's Entry/Exit System (EES) this week.
item.description:
Three things to know on the EU's Entry/Exit System (EES) this week.
1. Ryanair has named seven airports where EES is causing the worst queues: Tenerife South, Palma, Alicante, Malaga, Milan Bergamo, Krakow and Paris Beauvais. Passengers on Schengen routes are being advised to arrive earlier than usual.
2. Port of Dover declared a critical incident during May half-term, with waits of up to 4.5 hours. CEO Doug Bannister has warned of severe congestion through peak weeks, when volumes are expected to exceed 12,000 vehicles a day.
3. Greece has already suspended its EES rollout, and Ryanair is calling for a broader pause until September.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/ees-delays-eu-airports-summer-2026

item.title: Three things worth knowing this week.
item.description:
Three things worth knowing this week.
One: the FCDO lifted its UAE travel advice ban on 18 June, following the US-Iran peace agreement. Dubai is already back in agent enquiries.
Two: after a quieter June, agents are reporting a clearer uplift, with a late-market surge expected from mid-July as UK schools break.
Three: the FIFA World Cup 2026 is driving extraordinary last-minute bookings to US host cities, with agents flexing itineraries overnight as teams progress. Clients continue to travel around moments, and are leaning on agents harder to deliver them.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/uae-bookings-world-cup-2026-agent-outlook

item.title: The Canary Islands have launched REGNEXT, a voluntary tourism regeneration fund, choosing contribution over compulsion at a time when 18.39 million tourists visited the islands in 2025.
item.description:
The Canary Islands have launched REGNEXT, a voluntary tourism regeneration fund, choosing contribution over compulsion at a time when 18.39 million tourists visited the islands in 2025.
Three things worth noting:
1. It covers all seven islands and funds habitat restoration, affordable housing and climate adaptation through named pilot schemes, not a general pot.
2. easyJet Holidays, Jet2 and TUI are signed-up partners, so this is not a boutique initiative. It has mainstream distribution.
3. Clients asking sustainability questions before they book is increasingly the norm. Having a clear, accurate answer is part of what makes an agent consultation valuable.
Full article on the blog.
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/canary-islands-regnext-voluntary-tourism-fund

item.title: Virgin Voyages has dedicated the whole of July to its UK agent partners: triple rewards on bookings made this month, plus a "Bring on Mexico" fam trip giveaway.
item.description:
Virgin Voyages has dedicated the whole of July to its UK agent partners: triple rewards on bookings made this month, plus a "Bring on Mexico" fam trip giveaway.
Three things worth flagging:
1. Triple rewards across a full calendar month is more useful than a short window. Bookings that come in naturally across July all count.
2. The Mexico fam giveaway gives agents the opportunity to experience the itineraries first-hand. Personal experience of a product is the most effective way to sell it with genuine confidence.
3. Virgin Voyages' adult-only, contemporary positioning suits a specific client profile well. July is a reasonable moment to look closely if you have not yet.
Full article on the blog.
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/virgin-voyages-triple-rewards-mexico-fam-july-2026

item.title: A standout fam trip result from the independent sector this week, reported by Cruise Trade News.
item.description:
A standout fam trip result from the independent sector this week, reported by Cruise Trade News.
Three things worth knowing.
One, Lisa Holdroff, assistant manager at Newell's Travel in Falmouth, sailed on a 4-night Star Clippers fam from Malaga to Nice. Two, within two weeks of returning she had booked 7 passengers across several Star Clippers sailings, totalling £33,000. Three, the follow-up was multi-channel by design: a Falmouth Life magazine article, an agency window display, a blog on the Newell's website, and direct calls to existing clients.
Star Clippers is currently running reduced-rate agent sailings in July and August 2026.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/star-clippers-fam-trip-agent-booking

item.title: Three things worth knowing from Air Mauritius and the MTPA's June 2026 UK roadshow.
item.description:
Three things worth knowing from Air Mauritius and the MTPA's June 2026 UK roadshow.
One, the London Gatwick to SSR non-stop currently runs five times a week, with 159,164 UK seats on sale annually. Chief Commercial Officer Laurent Recoura has said he wants to grow to daily non-stop at the earliest opportunity.
Two, the fleet is expanding. Three additional A350-900s are on order, with the first arriving in December 2026, and business class has been extended from 28 to 32 seats.
Three, the destination story is being told under a 'slow luxury' banner, covering wellness, 15 golf courses, kitesurfing, diving and MICE, supported by the #WeareMauritius and Dodo campaigns. Air Mauritius will be at WTM London this November.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/air-mauritius-non-stop-london-gatwick

item.title: Travel Weekly is reporting a potential price "bloodbath" predicted in late summer.
item.description:
Travel Weekly is reporting a potential price "bloodbath" predicted in late summer. For agents, that single line is the most useful piece of market intelligence this week, and there are three reasons it matters this week.
First, the late-booking window is already active. UK agents are reporting late bookings building well into July, so a back-half price story doesn't slow demand, it sharpens it.
Second, it gives every agent a clean, honest line for the hesitating client. If a client is weighing up a late-summer booking, you can flag the Travel Weekly read, offer to hold an option for a week if they're flexible, and explain why locking in now is the safer play if they're not.
Third, it puts the agent ahead of the headline. By Friday at least one client will have seen the story in their feed. The agents who've already read the original report are the ones who sound confident when the phone rings.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/late-sales-build-uk-heatwave

item.title: Globetrender's 2027 Mediterranean outlook is out this week, and it's a useful read for any agent with luxury Med clients on the books.
item.description:
Globetrender's 2027 Mediterranean outlook is out this week, and it's a useful read for any agent with luxury Med clients on the books.
Three things worth knowing:
1. Micro Cultures are pulling luxury travellers towards Albania, Delos and the smaller Greek Islands, with honey-harvesting and ancient ruins replacing the headline sights.
2. Blue Mind Experiences are reshaping hotel design. MGallery Collection and NIKO Seaside Resort in Crete are building around water proximity, not just sea views.
3. The Comeback Effect matters most for repeat clients. 90% of European travellers are returning to familiar coastlines, but expecting unspoilt beaches, local bistros and winemaker visits on the second trip.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/mediterranean-luxury-travel-trends-2027

item.title: Riviera Travel has published its H1 2026 trade results, and the agent channel is doing the heavy lifting.
item.description:
Riviera Travel has published its H1 2026 trade results, and the agent channel is doing the heavy lifting.
Three things worth knowing:
1. Passengers booked through UK agents are up 27% year-on-year, with revenue through agents up 33%. River cruise leads with passenger numbers up 33% and revenue up 35%.
2. The solo programme is moving fast. Solo ship George Eliot is nearly 40% sold, and Riviera has indicated a second vessel in the river fleet could soon be aimed at solo travellers.
3. Two new agent-facing tools are now live. Riviera Explorer is the trade platform, and Riva AI is an AI assistant built to support sales and customer engagement.
Strong evidence of what trade-led growth looks like when supplier and agent are pulling in the same direction.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/riviera-travel-agent-success-2026

item.title: Cunard has hosted its first-ever dedicated homeworker fam trip aboard Queen Mary 2, sailing from Southampton to Le Havre and Cherbourg on 5 June 2026 with 21 homeworker travel agents on board.
item.description:
Cunard has hosted its first-ever dedicated homeworker fam trip aboard Queen Mary 2, sailing from Southampton to Le Havre and Cherbourg on 5 June 2026 with 21 homeworker travel agents on board.
Three things worth knowing:
1. Previous Cunard fam trips mixed trade channels. This one was designed specifically around homeworkers and how they sell. 2. Homeworker bookings have already driven more than £200,000 in group bookings into Cunard via the trade, including the JLT community. 3. Cunard's UK sales team has called the trip "long overdue" and confirmed more dedicated homeworker fams are planned.
QM2 remains the world's only ocean liner, and the crossings continue to attract independent luxury clients.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/cunard-queen-mary-2-homeworker-fam-trip

item.title: The UK's FCDO lifted its Gulf travel advisory on 18 June, covering the UAE, Qatar and Saudi Arabia. Australia moved the same day.
item.description:
The UK's FCDO lifted its Gulf travel advisory on 18 June, covering the UAE, Qatar and Saudi Arabia. Australia moved the same day.
Three things worth knowing for the trade right now:
1. UK carriers and operators are flying Gulf routes. Bookings to Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Doha, Riyadh and Jeddah can be confirmed against UK government guidance today.
2. EASA has not yet cleared Gulf airspace for European Union carriers. Lufthansa, Air France and KLM remain grounded on those routes, with clearance expected around October 2026.
3. UK agents currently hold a connectivity advantage their European counterparts do not. The FCDO page remains the live source of truth, and operator risk assessments should be checked alongside.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/gulf-travel-advisory-lifted-uk-agents

item.title: Tunisia returns to Jet2's programme from 1 May 2027, with eight weekly departures across five UK airports… Birmingham, East Midlands, Leeds Bradford, Manchester and Stansted, into Enfidha.
item.description:
Tunisia returns to Jet2's programme from 1 May 2027, with eight weekly departures across five UK airports… Birmingham, East Midlands, Leeds Bradford, Manchester and Stansted, into Enfidha.
Three things worth knowing for the trade:
One, Tunisia has been off Jet2's schedule since 2015, so this is genuinely new destination territory for many agents.
Two, it's package-led from launch, flights and hotel sold together, which means a full agent-bookable product from day one.
Three, Hammamet's resort infrastructure sits well below Spanish-island price points, useful for budget-conscious beach clients in 2027.
A quiet first-mover opportunity for agents who build the knowledge ahead of summer.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/jet2-tunisia-return-2027-agents

item.title: Qantas has confirmed Sydney to London as the first Project Sunrise route, launching October 2027.
item.description:
Qantas has confirmed Sydney to London as the first Project Sunrise route, launching October 2027.
Three things worth knowing for the trade:
1. It's the first non-stop between Australia's east coast and the UK, ending nearly 80 years of stopovers on the Kangaroo Route and saving up to four hours versus current journey times.
2. The aircraft is a custom Airbus A350-1000ULR with an additional 20,000-litre fuel tank for the 22-hour flight. 12 ordered, 238 seats across four cabins.
3. Tickets are expected on sale February 2027. Existing Perth-London and Sydney-Singapore-London services continue, and Sydney-New York is confirmed as the next Sunrise route.
Source: TTG Media, 17 June 2026.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
**First comment:** https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/qantas-confirms-sydney-london-non-stop-from-october-2027

item.title: A useful EES update for agents with clients travelling into the Schengen Area this summer.
item.description:
A useful EES update for agents with clients travelling into the Schengen Area this summer.
Three things worth knowing, per The Independent:
1. EU airports can temporarily pause the new biometric registration step if queues become unmanageable. Frontex confirmed the option at an ABTA conference this week, framed as a last resort.
2. UK travellers have been warned of waits up to 6 hours at peak times, with delays already reported in Portugal, Spain and Italy.
3. The flexibility ends in September. Almost 90 million entries and exits have been registered since October 2025.
Helpful context for agents advising clients on their first post-rollout trip into Schengen.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
**First comment:** https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/eu-border-queues-ees-suspension-summer

item.title: Consumer confidence in travel is up. The booking window has narrowed.
item.description:
Travel Weekly's exclusive consumer tracking data shows UK confidence in booking an overseas summer holiday has rallied significantly since late April 2026. The booking window, however, has narrowed.
Three things worth noting for agents this fortnight:
1. Intent is up. The proportion of people planning to travel in the next three months has risen.
2. Conversion is lagging. Clients are planning to travel soon and leaving the booking itself until later.
3. The opportunity sits in the gap. Confidence is the harder problem to solve, and that part has already moved. The nudge between intention and confirmation is where a named agent earns the booking.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/consumer-confidence-booking-windows-june-2026

item.title: Scotland fans heading to the 2026 FIFA World Cup are running into ESTA problems before they fly.
item.description:
Scotland fans heading to the 2026 FIFA World Cup are running into ESTA problems before they fly, and Travel Weekly is using the story as a live example of the value an agent brings.
Three things worth knowing:
1. Some Tartan Army ESTAs are being delayed or refused, and Scotland's First Minister has stepped in to help.
2. A refused ESTA closes the Visa Waiver door for that trip, pushing the traveller toward a full US visa application that takes weeks.
3. A Scottish agency has been credited with guiding one client through the issue calmly and in time.
This is the quiet, behind-the-scenes work that protects a client's trip long before they reach the airport.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/esta-world-cup-agents-june-2026

item.title: UK travel agencies are reporting one of their strongest weeks of trading this year.
item.description:
UK travel agencies are reporting one of their strongest weeks of trading this year, and the picture is worth a proper look.
Three things to know from this week's Travel Weekly reporting:
One, Barrhead Travel has called it their best year-on-year week since March, beating the spring peaks bounce in mid-June.
Two, the late-demand window has opened across the sector, with multiple agencies reporting the same shape of lift.
Three, the clients booking now are ready to book. The dithering is done, the dates are set, and the conversations are closing warmer and faster than earlier in the year.
A good signal for the agency channel heading into peak summer.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/travel-agencies-strong-trading-june-2026

item.title: Three things to know about Riyadh Air's launch this week.
item.description:
Three things to know about Riyadh Air's launch this week.
One: flight RX401 operated its first ever commercial service on 10 June 2026, Riyadh to London Heathrow, on a Boeing 787-9 Dreamliner. London is now daily.
Two: Manchester–Riyadh follows on 23 July 2026, three times weekly. Tickets are on sale now for both. The wider network already takes in Jeddah, Cairo, Dubai and Madrid.
Three: the carrier is backed by Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, with a stated ambition of 100+ destinations by 2030. Loyalty programme is called Sfeer.
Useful context for anyone advising clients on Saudi Arabia, the wider Middle East, or new long-haul carrier options.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment

item.title: Three things to know from the Caribbean this morning.
item.description:
Three things to know from the Caribbean this morning.
One, the St Kitts Tourism Authority has renewed its UK travel agent discount programme.
Two, the scheme includes discounted hotel rates at ten participating properties on the island, plus on-island benefits for agents who travel out in person.
Three, this is a renewal rather than a fresh launch, so the programme has a track record behind it and the tourist board is signalling continued investment in the UK trade.
A useful one to know about for agents with Caribbean enquiries on the desk.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment

item.title: New Citizens Advice research shows more than 4 million people in Britain encountered a problem with a package holiday in the past year. Three things worth knowing.
item.description:
New Citizens Advice research shows more than 4 million people in Britain encountered a problem with a package holiday in the past year. Three things worth knowing.
One, 34% of those who booked a package in the last twelve months reported an issue, and the charity now takes around 14 complaints a day.
Two, 42% of the complaints came from all-inclusive trips booked abroad, with quality issues and customer service failures the largest categories.
Three, 1 in 4 affected travellers said the experience left them stressed or upset, and 17% paid extra to get through.
For travel agents, a useful reminder of where professional support earns its keep.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/package-holiday-problems-why-an-agent-matters

item.title: Oceania Cruises has put trade product knowledge in the spotlight, and the results are worth flagging.
item.description:
Oceania Cruises has put trade product knowledge in the spotlight, and the results are worth flagging.
Three things to know:
1. The line ran a UK trade competition to drive engagement, and walked away impressed by the depth of product knowledge across agents. Clare Sacco took the headline win.
2. Oceania's sales leadership has pledged to expand its fam trip programme so more agents can experience the ships in person.
3. For agencies selling premium destination-immersive cruise, this signals continued investment in agent expertise.
A useful read-through for anyone selling cruise at the top end of the market.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/oceania-cruises-competition-strength-of-knowledge

item.title: Clia UK 2026 wrapped in Southampton this week, with more than 800 delegates in the room.
item.description:
Clia UK 2026 wrapped in Southampton this week, with more than 800 delegates in the room.
Three things worth knowing for UK travel agents selling cruise this summer:
One, senior cruise line leadership identified UK agents as a "great opportunity" to help fill late-booking capacity over the coming months.
Two, the wider picture is global. The Middle East conflict is still affecting international cruise markets, softening demand from some traditional regions.
Three, the UK trade has held up well by contrast, which is why the cruise lines are looking towards British sellers for the late availability.
Worth keeping in mind when you next scan your active cruise enquiries.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/cruise-late-capacity-opportunity-uk-agents

item.title: Silversea Cruises has launched a UK trade referral initiative paying travel agents £100 for every new-to-brand client they introduce who then books a Silversea cruise.
item.description:
Silversea Cruises has launched a UK trade referral initiative paying travel agents £100 for every new-to-brand client they introduce who then books a Silversea cruise.
Three things worth knowing:
1. The window is short. It runs from 1 June to 31 July 2026.
2. It is targeted. The £100 is payable only for first-time Silversea clients, not existing guests.
3. The brief is simple. No tiers, no thresholds. One new client, one booking, one referral fee.
Silversea sits at the ultra-luxury end of the cruise market and has been part of the Royal Caribbean Group since 2018. Source: Travel Weekly.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/silversea-100-referral-initiative-uk-agents

item.title: Three things to know about Qantas' Project Sunrise this week.
item.description:
Three things to know about Qantas' Project Sunrise this week.
One: the Airbus A350-1000ULR completed its maiden test flight on 2 June from Toulouse, a 3 hour 43 minute sortie with Airbus' flight test team on board.
Two: around 80 hours of flight testing now begins across roughly two months, including certification of a new lighter galley air cooling system that will roll out across all future Qantas A350s.
Three: Qantas has confirmed it will announce the first Project Sunrise route and timing of inaugural services later this month, with Sydney to London and Sydney to New York both in scope.
Full blog on the website link in the first comment
First comment: https://janinelovestravel.co.uk/qantas-project-sunrise-first-flight-london-sydney
